seoTitle: Export Data From QuickBooks Online to Excel
metaDescription: How to export financial reports and master lists like customers and vendors from QuickBooks Online into clean Excel spreadsheets.
intro: Whether you're prepping for tax season, running a deep-dive analysis, or building a custom pivot table, QuickBooks Online can push your reports and master lists straight into Excel without messy copy-paste formatting. This guide covers both exporting individual reports and bulk-downloading entire data lists.

steps.heading: Open a financial report
steps.body: Go to Reports from the sidebar and search for a statement like Profit and Loss or Balance Sheet to open its grid view.

steps.heading: Set your date range and method
steps.body: Use the customization ribbon to set the reporting period and choose between Cash and Accrual before running the report.

steps.heading: Export to Excel
steps.body: Click Export/Share in the upper right of the report, then choose Export to Excel to download a formatted spreadsheet.

steps.heading: Export master lists
steps.body: For bulk data like Customers, Vendors, or the Chart of Accounts, click the Gear icon and select Export Data under Tools.

steps.heading: Verify the download
steps.body: Open your Downloads folder to confirm the file unzipped correctly and that the data parses cleanly inside Excel.

faq.question: Can I export more than one report at a time?
faq.answer: Individual reports export one at a time, but the master list export tool lets you batch-download multiple list types like customers and vendors together.

faq.question: Will my custom report filters carry over to the Excel file?
faq.answer: Yes, whatever date range and customization settings you applied before running the report are reflected in the exported spreadsheet.

faq.question: Why did my browser block the download?
faq.answer: Some browsers block automatic downloads by default — check your popup or download settings if the export doesn't appear.

faq.question: Does the export preserve formatting like bold totals?
faq.answer: QuickBooks aims to keep multi-row nested formatting intact when exporting, so totals and subtotals typically carry over.
